revision.text:
'''Kenmon System''' (権門体制, ''Kenmon System Theory'') is a historical theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]], describing the political structure of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. This theory was first introduced in 1963 in the Iwanami Lecture Series ''Japanese History: Middle Ages 2'' under the title ''The Medieval State and the Emperor''.
Historian {{ill|Akira Imatani|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Imatani|qq=}} has pointed out that Kuroda's ''Kenmon System Theory'' inherits elements from the pre-war research of [[:en:Hiraizumi Kiyoshi|Sumio Hiraizumi]].
== Overview == Previously, medieval Japan was broadly understood as a society in which the old aristocratic power represented by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], religious authorities (temples), and emerging samurai power were in constant three-way conflict.
In contrast, Kuroda coined the term {{ill|Kenmon Seika|ja|権門勢家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon Seika|qq=}}, developing the theory of the ''Kenmon System''.
''Kenmon Seika'' can be simply described as influential political and economic forces possessing both authority and power. These included: * '''Aristocratic Kenmon''' (''Shissei'', 執政) – governing authority * '''Religious Kenmon''' (''Goji'', 護持) – spiritual guardianship * '''Samurai Kenmon''' (''Shugo'', 守護) – military protection
Each of these factions sustained their power through economic bases such as the [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (manorial estates). While conflicts existed, they also maintained a complementary relationship, effectively functioning as a divided system of governance. According to the theory, various state functions were entrusted to each faction’s family-based administrative system, while the Emperor conferred official Ranks to unify and mediate between the three powers. In this sense, the Emperor was positioned at the pinnacle of the Kenmon structure, akin to the sovereign of a feudal state.
Kuroda argued that the ''Kenmon System'' collapsed with the decline of the shōen system, particularly after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] (1467–1477). From then until the unification of Japan under the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|織豊政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|qq=}}, centralized state power effectively ceased to exist.
=== The Term "Royal House" (王家) as a Historical Concept === Kuroda introduced the term {{ill|Ōke|ja|王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōke|qq=}} ("Royal House") to describe the medieval Japanese imperial family, aligning it with the terms used for the aristocracy (公家), samurai (武家), and temple authorities (寺家).<ref name="Hongo">Kazuto Hongo, ''Nazotoki Taira no Kiyomori'' (20dd, Bungeishunjū), pp. 63–64.</ref><ref name="Okano">Tomohiko Okano, ''The Minamoto Clan and the Japanese Monarch'' (2003, Kōdansha Gendai Shinsho), pp. 81–82.</ref><ref name="Takamatsu">"The History of Scholarship on the Term 'Ōke'," ''Rekishi Hyōron'' (August 20dd).</ref><ref name="Special">Editorial Committee of ''Rekishi Hyōron'', "The Present State of Ōke Studies in the Insei Period: Introduction to the Special Issue" (''Rekishi Hyōron'', August 20dd).</ref>
Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|qq=}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|qq=}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|qq=}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|qq=}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|qq=}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|qq=}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|qq=}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>

In this sense, the Emperor was positioned at the pinnacle of the Kenmon structure, akin to the sovereign of a feudal state.
Kuroda argued that the ''Kenmon System'' collapsed with the decline of the shōen system, particularly after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] (1467–1477). From then until the unification of Japan under the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|織豊政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|qq=}}, centralized state power effectively ceased to exist.
=== The Term "Royal House" (王家) as a Historical Concept === Kuroda introduced the term {{ill|Ōke|ja|王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōke|qq=}} ("Royal House") to describe the medieval Japanese imperial family, aligning it with the terms used for the aristocracy (公家), samurai (武家), and temple authorities (寺家).<ref name="Hongo">Kazuto Hongo, ''Nazotoki Taira no Kiyomori'' (20dd, Bungeishunjū), pp. 63–64.</ref><ref name="Okano">Tomohiko Okano, ''The Minamoto Clan and the Japanese Monarch'' (2003, Kōdansha Gendai Shinsho), pp. 81–82.</ref><ref name="Takamatsu">"The History of Scholarship on the Term 'Ōke'," ''Rekishi Hyōron'' (August 20dd).</ref><ref name="Special">Editorial Committee of ''Rekishi Hyōron'', "The Present State of Ōke Studies in the Insei Period: Introduction to the Special Issue" (''Rekishi Hyōron'', August 20dd).</ref>
Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|qq=}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|qq=}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|qq=}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|qq=}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|qq=}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|qq=}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|qq=}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>

'''Kenmon System''' (権門体制, ''Kenmon System Theory'') is a historical theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]], describing the political structure of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. This theory was first introduced in 1963 in the Iwanami Lecture Series ''Japanese History: Middle Ages 2'' under the title ''The Medieval State and the Emperor''.
Historian {{ill|Akira Imatani|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Imatani|qq=}} has pointed out that Kuroda's ''Kenmon System Theory'' inherits elements from the pre-war research of [[:en:Hiraizumi Kiyoshi|Sumio Hiraizumi]].
== Overview == Previously, medieval Japan was broadly understood as a society in which the old aristocratic power represented by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], religious authorities (temples), and emerging samurai power were in constant three-way conflict.
In contrast, Kuroda coined the term {{ill|Kenmon Seika|ja|権門勢家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon Seika|qq=}}, developing the theory of the ''Kenmon System''.
''Kenmon Seika'' can be simply described as influential political and economic forces possessing both authority and power. These included: * '''Aristocratic Kenmon''' (''Shissei'', 執政) – governing authority * '''Religious Kenmon''' (''Goji'', 護持) – spiritual guardianship * '''Samurai Kenmon''' (''Shugo'', 守護) – military protection
Each of these factions sustained their power through economic bases such as the [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (manorial estates). While conflicts existed, they also maintained a complementary relationship, effectively functioning as a divided system of governance. According to the theory, various state functions were entrusted to each faction’s family-based administrative system, while the Emperor conferred official Ranks to unify and mediate between the three powers. In this sense, the Emperor was positioned at the pinnacle of the Kenmon structure, akin to the sovereign of a feudal state.
Kuroda argued that the ''Kenmon System'' collapsed with the decline of the shōen system, particularly after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] (1467–1477). From then until the unification of Japan under the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|織豊政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|qq=}}, centralized state power effectively ceased to exist.
=== The Term "Royal House" (王家) as a Historical Concept === Kuroda introduced the term {{ill|Ōke|ja|王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōke|qq=}} ("Royal House") to describe the medieval Japanese imperial family, aligning it with the terms used for the aristocracy (公家), samurai (武家), and temple authorities (寺家).<ref name="Hongo">Kazuto Hongo, ''Nazotoki Taira no Kiyomori'' (20dd, Bungeishunjū), pp. 63–64.</ref><ref name="Okano">Tomohiko Okano, ''The Minamoto Clan and the Japanese Monarch'' (2003, Kōdansha Gendai Shinsho), pp. 81–82.</ref><ref name="Takamatsu">"The History of Scholarship on the Term 'Ōke'," ''Rekishi Hyōron'' (August 20dd).</ref><ref name="Special">Editorial Committee of ''Rekishi Hyōron'', "The Present State of Ōke Studies in the Insei Period: Introduction to the Special Issue" (''Rekishi Hyōron'', August 20dd).</ref>
Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|qq=}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|qq=}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|qq=}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|qq=}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|qq=}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|qq=}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>

'''Kenmon System''' (権門体制, ''Kenmon System Theory'') is a historical theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]], describing the political structure of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. This theory was first introduced in 1963 in the Iwanami Lecture Series ''Japanese History: Middle Ages 2'' under the title ''The Medieval State and the Emperor''.
Historian {{ill|Akira Imatani|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Imatani|qq=}} has pointed out that Kuroda's ''Kenmon System Theory'' inherits elements from the pre-war research of [[:en:Hiraizumi Kiyoshi|Sumio Hiraizumi]].
== Overview == Previously, medieval Japan was broadly understood as a society in which the old aristocratic power represented by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], religious authorities (temples), and emerging samurai power were in constant three-way conflict.
In contrast, Kuroda coined the term {{ill|Kenmon Seika|ja|権門勢家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon Seika|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門}}, developing the theory of the ''Kenmon System''.
''Kenmon Seika'' can be simply described as influential political and economic forces possessing both authority and power. These included: * '''Aristocratic Kenmon''' (''Shissei'', 執政) – governing authority * '''Religious Kenmon''' (''Goji'', 護持) – spiritual guardianship * '''Samurai Kenmon''' (''Shugo'', 守護) – military protection
Each of these factions sustained their power through economic bases such as the [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (manorial estates). While conflicts existed, they also maintained a complementary relationship, effectively functioning as a divided system of governance. According to the theory, various state functions were entrusted to each faction’s family-based administrative system, while the Emperor conferred official Ranks to unify and mediate between the three powers. In this sense, the Emperor was positioned at the pinnacle of the Kenmon structure, akin to the sovereign of a feudal state.
Kuroda argued that the ''Kenmon System'' collapsed with the decline of the shōen system, particularly after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] (1467–1477). From then until the unification of Japan under the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|織豊政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安土桃山時代}}, centralized state power effectively ceased to exist.
=== The Term "Royal House" (王家) as a Historical Concept === Kuroda introduced the term {{ill|Ōke|ja|王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōke|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 王族}} ("Royal House") to describe the medieval Japanese imperial family, aligning it with the terms used for the aristocracy (公家), samurai (武家), and temple authorities (寺家).<ref name="Hongo">Kazuto Hongo, ''Nazotoki Taira no Kiyomori'' (20dd, Bungeishunjū), pp. 63–64.</ref><ref name="Okano">Tomohiko Okano, ''The Minamoto Clan and the Japanese Monarch'' (2003, Kōdansha Gendai Shinsho), pp. 81–82.</ref><ref name="Takamatsu">"The History of Scholarship on the Term 'Ōke'," ''Rekishi Hyōron'' (August 20dd).</ref><ref name="Special">Editorial Committee of ''Rekishi Hyōron'', "The Present State of Ōke Studies in the Insei Period: Introduction to the Special Issue" (''Rekishi Hyōron'', August 20dd).</ref>
Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|qq=}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|qq=}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|qq=}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|qq=}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|qq=}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|qq=}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|qq=}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:日本の歴史論争]] into [[Category:Historical Controversies of Japan]] via bot
revision.text:
'''Kenmon System''' (権門体制, ''Kenmon System Theory'') is a historical theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]], describing the political structure of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. This theory was first introduced in 1963 in the Iwanami Lecture Series ''Japanese History: Middle Ages 2'' under the title ''The Medieval State and the Emperor''.
Historian {{ill|Akira Imatani|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Imatani|qq=}} has pointed out that Kuroda's ''Kenmon System Theory'' inherits elements from the pre-war research of [[:en:Hiraizumi Kiyoshi|Sumio Hiraizumi]].
== Overview == Previously, medieval Japan was broadly understood as a society in which the old aristocratic power represented by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], religious authorities (temples), and emerging samurai power were in constant three-way conflict.
In contrast, Kuroda coined the term {{ill|Kenmon Seika|ja|権門勢家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon Seika|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門}}, developing the theory of the ''Kenmon System''.
''Kenmon Seika'' can be simply described as influential political and economic forces possessing both authority and power. These included: * '''Aristocratic Kenmon''' (''Shissei'', 執政) – governing authority * '''Religious Kenmon''' (''Goji'', 護持) – spiritual guardianship * '''Samurai Kenmon''' (''Shugo'', 守護) – military protection
Each of these factions sustained their power through economic bases such as the [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (manorial estates). While conflicts existed, they also maintained a complementary relationship, effectively functioning as a divided system of governance. According to the theory, various state functions were entrusted to each faction’s family-based administrative system, while the Emperor conferred official Ranks to unify and mediate between the three powers. In this sense, the Emperor was positioned at the pinnacle of the Kenmon structure, akin to the sovereign of a feudal state.
Kuroda argued that the ''Kenmon System'' collapsed with the decline of the shōen system, particularly after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] (1467–1477). From then until the unification of Japan under the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|織豊政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安土桃山時代}}, centralized state power effectively ceased to exist.
=== The Term "Royal House" (王家) as a Historical Concept === Kuroda introduced the term {{ill|Ōke|ja|王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōke|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 王族}} ("Royal House") to describe the medieval Japanese imperial family, aligning it with the terms used for the aristocracy (公家), samurai (武家), and temple authorities (寺家).<ref name="Hongo">Kazuto Hongo, ''Nazotoki Taira no Kiyomori'' (20dd, Bungeishunjū), pp. 63–64.</ref><ref name="Okano">Tomohiko Okano, ''The Minamoto Clan and the Japanese Monarch'' (2003, Kōdansha Gendai Shinsho), pp. 81–82.</ref><ref name="Takamatsu">"The History of Scholarship on the Term 'Ōke'," ''Rekishi Hyōron'' (August 20dd).</ref><ref name="Special">Editorial Committee of ''Rekishi Hyōron'', "The Present State of Ōke Studies in the Insei Period: Introduction to the Special Issue" (''Rekishi Hyōron'', August 20dd).</ref>
Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|qq=}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|qq=}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|qq=}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|qq=}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|qq=}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|qq=}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|qq=}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
* [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]]
* {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}}
* {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sōhei|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōhei|qq=}}
* {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}

Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|qq=}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|qq=}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|qq=}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|qq=}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|qq=}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|qq=}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>

[[sn:Kenmon System Theory]] [[ja:権門体制]] [[ja:アジール]] [[ja:境内都市]] [[ja:寺内町]] [[ja:僧兵]] [[ja:神人]] [[ko:%EA%B6%8C%EB%AC%B8%EC%B2%B4%EC%A0%9C]] [[ja:寺社勢力]]
<!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--><!--Kenmon System Theory--> [[Category:Historiography of Japan]] [[Category:Historical Controversies of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:권문체제]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11542344]] Wikidata name: kenmon system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11542344]] Wikidata name: kenmon system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmon System''' (権門体制, ''Kenmon System Theory'') is a historical theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]], describing the political structure of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. This theory was first introduced in 1963 in the Iwanami Lecture Series ''Japanese History: Middle Ages 2'' under the title ''The Medieval State and the Emperor''.
Historian {{ill|Akira Imatani|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Imatani|qq=|1=Akira Imatani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377407}} has pointed out that Kuroda's ''Kenmon System Theory'' inherits elements from the pre-war research of [[:en:Hiraizumi Kiyoshi|Sumio Hiraizumi]].
== Overview == Previously, medieval Japan was broadly understood as a society in which the old aristocratic power represented by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], religious authorities (temples), and emerging samurai power were in constant three-way conflict.
In contrast, Kuroda coined the term {{ill|Kenmon Seika|ja|権門勢家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon Seika|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門|1=kenmon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110160911}}, developing the theory of the ''Kenmon System''.
''Kenmon Seika'' can be simply described as influential political and economic forces possessing both authority and power. These included: * '''Aristocratic Kenmon''' (''Shissei'', 執政) – governing authority * '''Religious Kenmon''' (''Goji'', 護持) – spiritual guardianship * '''Samurai Kenmon''' (''Shugo'', 守護) – military protection
Each of these factions sustained their power through economic bases such as the [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (manorial estates). While conflicts existed, they also maintained a complementary relationship, effectively functioning as a divided system of governance. According to the theory, various state functions were entrusted to each faction’s family-based administrative system, while the Emperor conferred official Ranks to unify and mediate between the three powers. In this sense, the Emperor was positioned at the pinnacle of the Kenmon structure, akin to the sovereign of a feudal state.
Kuroda argued that the ''Kenmon System'' collapsed with the decline of the shōen system, particularly after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] (1467–1477). From then until the unification of Japan under the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|織豊政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安土桃山時代|1=Azuchi-Momoyama period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q319531}}, centralized state power effectively ceased to exist.
=== The Term "Royal House" (王家) as a Historical Concept === Kuroda introduced the term {{ill|Ōke|ja|王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōke|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 王族|1=royalty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573099}} ("Royal House") to describe the medieval Japanese imperial family, aligning it with the terms used for the aristocracy (公家), samurai (武家), and temple authorities (寺家).<ref name="Hongo">Kazuto Hongo, ''Nazotoki Taira no Kiyomori'' (20dd, Bungeishunjū), pp. 63–64.</ref><ref name="Okano">Tomohiko Okano, ''The Minamoto Clan and the Japanese Monarch'' (2003, Kōdansha Gendai Shinsho), pp. 81–82.</ref><ref name="Takamatsu">"The History of Scholarship on the Term 'Ōke'," ''Rekishi Hyōron'' (August 20dd).</ref><ref name="Special">Editorial Committee of ''Rekishi Hyōron'', "The Present State of Ōke Studies in the Insei Period: Introduction to the Special Issue" (''Rekishi Hyōron'', August 20dd).</ref>
Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|qq=|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642212}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|qq=|1=Takahiro Okuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446170}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|qq=|1=tōgoku kokka theory|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526385}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|qq=|1=Shin'ichi Satō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11384498}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|qq=|1=Fumihiko Gomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372248}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|qq=|1=Chiten no kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554403}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|qq=|1=Muneo Sasaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248713}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:権門体制]][[ko:권문체제]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11542344]] Wikidata name: kenmon system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11542344]] Wikidata name: kenmon system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmon System''' (権門体制, ''Kenmon System Theory'') is a historical theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]], describing the political structure of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. This theory was first introduced in 1963 in the Iwanami Lecture Series ''Japanese History: Middle Ages 2'' under the title ''The Medieval State and the Emperor''.
Historian {{ill|Akira Imatani|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Imatani|qq=|1=Akira Imatani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377407}} has pointed out that Kuroda's ''Kenmon System Theory'' inherits elements from the pre-war research of [[:en:Hiraizumi Kiyoshi|Sumio Hiraizumi]].
== Overview == Previously, medieval Japan was broadly understood as a society in which the old aristocratic power represented by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], religious authorities (temples), and emerging samurai power were in constant three-way conflict.
In contrast, Kuroda coined the term {{ill|Kenmon Seika|ja|権門勢家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon Seika|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門|1=kenmon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110160911}}, developing the theory of the ''Kenmon System''.
''Kenmon Seika'' can be simply described as influential political and economic forces possessing both authority and power. These included: * '''Aristocratic Kenmon''' (''Shissei'', 執政) – governing authority * '''Religious Kenmon''' (''Goji'', 護持) – spiritual guardianship * '''Samurai Kenmon''' (''Shugo'', 守護) – military protection
Each of these factions sustained their power through economic bases such as the [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (manorial estates). While conflicts existed, they also maintained a complementary relationship, effectively functioning as a divided system of governance. According to the theory, various state functions were entrusted to each faction’s family-based administrative system, while the Emperor conferred official Ranks to unify and mediate between the three powers. In this sense, the Emperor was positioned at the pinnacle of the Kenmon structure, akin to the sovereign of a feudal state.
Kuroda argued that the ''Kenmon System'' collapsed with the decline of the shōen system, particularly after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] (1467–1477). From then until the unification of Japan under the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|織豊政権|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安土桃山時代|1=Azuchi-Momoyama period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q319531}}, centralized state power effectively ceased to exist.
=== The Term "Royal House" (王家) as a Historical Concept === Kuroda introduced the term {{ill|Ōke|ja|王家|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōke|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 王族|1=royalty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573099}} ("Royal House") to describe the medieval Japanese imperial family, aligning it with the terms used for the aristocracy (公家), samurai (武家), and temple authorities (寺家).<ref name="Hongo">Kazuto Hongo, ''Nazotoki Taira no Kiyomori'' (20dd, Bungeishunjū), pp. 63–64.</ref><ref name="Okano">Tomohiko Okano, ''The Minamoto Clan and the Japanese Monarch'' (2003, Kōdansha Gendai Shinsho), pp. 81–82.</ref><ref name="Takamatsu">"The History of Scholarship on the Term 'Ōke'," ''Rekishi Hyōron'' (August 20dd).</ref><ref name="Special">Editorial Committee of ''Rekishi Hyōron'', "The Present State of Ōke Studies in the Insei Period: Introduction to the Special Issue" (''Rekishi Hyōron'', August 20dd).</ref>
Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|qq=|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642212}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|qq=|1=Takahiro Okuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446170}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|qq=|1=tōgoku kokka theory|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526385}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|qq=|1=Shin'ichi Satō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11384498}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|qq=|1=Fumihiko Gomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372248}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|qq=|1=Chiten no kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554403}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|qq=|1=sasaki muneo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248713}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>

* {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}}
* {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sōhei|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōhei|qq=}}
* {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}

Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642212}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|1=Takahiro Okuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446170}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|1=tōgoku kokka theory|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526385}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|1=Shin'ichi Satō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11384498}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|1=Fumihiko Gomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372248}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|1=Chiten no kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554403}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|1=sasaki muneo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248713}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:権門体制]][[ko:권문체제]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11542344]] Wikidata name: kenmon system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11542344]] Wikidata name: kenmon system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmon System''' (権門体制, ''Kenmon System Theory'') is a historical theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]], describing the political structure of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. This theory was first introduced in 1963 in the Iwanami Lecture Series ''Japanese History: Middle Ages 2'' under the title ''The Medieval State and the Emperor''.
Historian {{ill|Akira Imatani|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Imatani|1=Akira Imatani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377407}} has pointed out that Kuroda's ''Kenmon System Theory'' inherits elements from the pre-war research of [[:en:Hiraizumi Kiyoshi|Sumio Hiraizumi]].
== Overview == Previously, medieval Japan was broadly understood as a society in which the old aristocratic power represented by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], religious authorities (temples), and emerging samurai power were in constant three-way conflict.
In contrast, Kuroda coined the term {{ill|Kenmon Seika|ja|権門勢家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon Seika|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門|1=kenmon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110160911}}, developing the theory of the ''Kenmon System''.
''Kenmon Seika'' can be simply described as influential political and economic forces possessing both authority and power. These included: * '''Aristocratic Kenmon''' (''Shissei'', 執政) – governing authority * '''Religious Kenmon''' (''Goji'', 護持) – spiritual guardianship * '''Samurai Kenmon''' (''Shugo'', 守護) – military protection
Each of these factions sustained their power through economic bases such as the [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (manorial estates). While conflicts existed, they also maintained a complementary relationship, effectively functioning as a divided system of governance. According to the theory, various state functions were entrusted to each faction’s family-based administrative system, while the Emperor conferred official Ranks to unify and mediate between the three powers. In this sense, the Emperor was positioned at the pinnacle of the Kenmon structure, akin to the sovereign of a feudal state.
Kuroda argued that the ''Kenmon System'' collapsed with the decline of the shōen system, particularly after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] (1467–1477). From then until the unification of Japan under the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|織豊政権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安土桃山時代|1=Azuchi-Momoyama period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q319531}}, centralized state power effectively ceased to exist.
=== The Term "Royal House" (王家) as a Historical Concept === Kuroda introduced the term {{ill|Ōke|ja|王家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōke|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 王族|1=royalty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573099}} ("Royal House") to describe the medieval Japanese imperial family, aligning it with the terms used for the aristocracy (公家), samurai (武家), and temple authorities (寺家).<ref name="Hongo">Kazuto Hongo, ''Nazotoki Taira no Kiyomori'' (20dd, Bungeishunjū), pp. 63–64.</ref><ref name="Okano">Tomohiko Okano, ''The Minamoto Clan and the Japanese Monarch'' (2003, Kōdansha Gendai Shinsho), pp. 81–82.</ref><ref name="Takamatsu">"The History of Scholarship on the Term 'Ōke'," ''Rekishi Hyōron'' (August 20dd).</ref><ref name="Special">Editorial Committee of ''Rekishi Hyōron'', "The Present State of Ōke Studies in the Insei Period: Introduction to the Special Issue" (''Rekishi Hyōron'', August 20dd).</ref>
Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642212}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|1=Takahiro Okuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446170}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|1=tōgoku kokka theory|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526385}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|1=Shin'ichi Satō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11384498}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|1=Fumihiko Gomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372248}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|1=Chiten no kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554403}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|1=sasaki muneo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248713}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:権門体制]][[ko:권문체제]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11542344]] Wikidata name: kenmon system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11542344]] Wikidata name: kenmon system [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenmon System''' (権門体制, ''Kenmon System Theory'') is a historical theory proposed by historian [[:en:Toshio Kuroda (Shinto professor)|Toshio Kuroda]], describing the political structure of medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. This theory was first introduced in 1963 in the Iwanami Lecture Series ''Japanese History: Middle Ages 2'' under the title ''The Medieval State and the Emperor''.
Historian {{ill|Akira Imatani|ja|今谷明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akira Imatani|1=Akira Imatani|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377407}} has pointed out that Kuroda's ''Kenmon System Theory'' inherits elements from the pre-war research of [[:en:Hiraizumi Kiyoshi|Sumio Hiraizumi]].
== Overview == Previously, medieval Japan was broadly understood as a society in which the old aristocratic power represented by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], religious authorities (temples), and emerging samurai power were in constant three-way conflict.
In contrast, Kuroda coined the term {{ill|Kenmon Seika|ja|権門勢家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon Seika|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 権門|1=kenmon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q110160911}}, developing the theory of the ''Kenmon System''.
''Kenmon Seika'' can be simply described as influential political and economic forces possessing both authority and power. These included: * '''Aristocratic Kenmon''' (''Shissei'', 執政) – governing authority * '''Religious Kenmon''' (''Goji'', 護持) – spiritual guardianship * '''Samurai Kenmon''' (''Shugo'', 守護) – military protection
Each of these factions sustained their power through economic bases such as the [[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]] (manorial estates). While conflicts existed, they also maintained a complementary relationship, effectively functioning as a divided system of governance. According to the theory, various state functions were entrusted to each faction’s family-based administrative system, while the Emperor conferred official Ranks to unify and mediate between the three powers. In this sense, the Emperor was positioned at the pinnacle of the Kenmon structure, akin to the sovereign of a feudal state.
Kuroda argued that the ''Kenmon System'' collapsed with the decline of the shōen system, particularly after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] (1467–1477). From then until the unification of Japan under the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|織豊政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安土桃山時代|1=Azuchi-Momoyama period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q319531}}, centralized state power effectively ceased to exist.
=== The Term "Royal House" (王家) as a Historical Concept === Kuroda introduced the term {{ill|Ōke|ja|王家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōke|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 王族|1=royalty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573099}} ("Royal House") to describe the medieval Japanese imperial family, aligning it with the terms used for the aristocracy (公家), samurai (武家), and temple authorities (寺家).<ref name="Hongo">Kazuto Hongo, ''Nazotoki Taira no Kiyomori'' (20dd, Bungeishunjū), pp. 63–64.</ref><ref name="Okano">Tomohiko Okano, ''The Minamoto Clan and the Japanese Monarch'' (2003, Kōdansha Gendai Shinsho), pp. 81–82.</ref><ref name="Takamatsu">"The History of Scholarship on the Term 'Ōke'," ''Rekishi Hyōron'' (August 20dd).</ref><ref name="Special">Editorial Committee of ''Rekishi Hyōron'', "The Present State of Ōke Studies in the Insei Period: Introduction to the Special Issue" (''Rekishi Hyōron'', August 20dd).</ref>
Kuroda argued that the term "Imperial Household" (皇室) was an ideological construct promoted by the modern Meiji government to reinforce the concept of unbroken imperial lineage. He viewed the use of "Royal House" (王家) as a neutral, academic alternative.<ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Asa">Toshio Kuroda, "Thoughts on the Terms 朝家・皇家・皇室," ''Nihon Rekishi'' (March 1982).</ref> Kuroda positioned the medieval imperial family as one of the Kenmon factions, coexisting with the aristocracy, samurai, and religious institutions.<ref name="HongoP36-38">Hongo, pp. 36–38.</ref>
According to Kuroda, the "Royal House" in medieval Japan was: :''Not a single-family structure under an emperor acting as patriarch, as implied in the old Imperial Household Law. Rather, it encompassed multiple semi-autonomous factions (院・宮), forming a broader kinship network.''<ref name="Asa"/>
However, historians such as {{ill|Mototaka Endo|ja|遠藤基郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mototaka Endo|1=Motoo Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642212}} have noted that Kuroda’s concept of "Royal House" does not always align with how the term appears in historical sources. Endo likens it to a "dialect" used within specific research circles.<ref name="Endo">Mototaka Endo, ''The Structure of Royal Authority in the Insei Period,'' pp. 1–7.</ref>
Kuroda's proposal sparked debate, particularly in the early 1980s, when historian {{ill|Takahiro Okuno|ja|奥野高廣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahiro Okuno|1=Takahiro Okuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446170}} criticized it, arguing that the terms "Imperial Family" (皇家) and "Imperial Household" (皇室) were already in use during the medieval period.<ref name="Takamatsu"/><ref name="Okano"/><ref name="Special"/>
=== Criticism === The ''Kenmon System'' theory has faced significant criticism, particularly from proponents of the {{ill|Eastern State Theory|ja|東国国家論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern State Theory|1=tōgoku kokka theory|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526385}}, spearheaded by historian {{ill|Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|ja|佐藤進一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin'ichi Satō (historian)|1=Shin'ichi Satō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11384498}}. This perspective asserts that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] functioned as an independent medieval state in eastern Japan, distinct from the imperial court in the west. It posits that after 1252, when the shogunate invited a prince as its nominal ruler, it aimed to solidify its autonomy from the western imperial authority.
Historian {{ill|Fumihiko Gomi|ja|五味文彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fumihiko Gomi|1=Fumihiko Gomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372248}} proposed an intermediary theory known as the ''Dual Kingship Theory'' (二つの王権論). According to this model, Japan during the medieval period consisted of two ruling powers: - The '''Western King''', represented by the Emperor. - The '''Eastern King''', represented by the Shogun.<ref>[https://www.hi.u-tokyo.ac.jp/personal/kazuto/shohyou/yamazaki.html "Masakazu Yamazaki’s ''Muromachi Chronicle'' - Exploring the Position of Yamazaki Historiography"] Tokyo University.</ref>
Additionally, even within ''Kenmon System'' scholarship, there have been modifications, such as: - Proposals that the true sovereign was not the Emperor but the {{ill|Chiten no Kimi|ja|治天の君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiten no Kimi|1=Chiten no kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554403}} (Retired Emperor acting as de facto ruler).<ref>Masahiro Tomita, Akira Imatani.</ref> - Suggestions that the ''Kenmon System'' was only applicable until the early Kamakura period.<ref>Masayoshi Ueyokote.</ref>
Historian {{ill|Muneo Sasaki|ja|佐々木宗雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muneo Sasaki|1=sasaki muneo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248713}} has criticized the ''Kenmon System'' for being an untested hypothesis that has gained unwarranted acceptance. He argues that the theory lacks direct evidence demonstrating the role of the Emperor as a Kenmon authority, and that it was the aristocratic families and temples, not the Emperor, who directly controlled land and people.<ref>Muneo Sasaki, ''The National Structure of Medieval Japan'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2018), pp. 13, 150–151, 182.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
* [[:en:Right of asylum|Right of asylum]]
* {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}}
* {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sōhei|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōhei|qq=}}
* {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}
